title_ar: باب ما جاء فيمن لم يقنع بالحلف بالله
content_en:
The concluding section of the core doctrinal chapters in Kitab al-Tawhid addresses the various actions and beliefs that, if present, negate or severely compromise a person's Tawhid. This is an essential counterpart to the chapters on Tawhid's virtue and meaning — knowing what destroys Tawhid is as important as knowing what constitutes it.

The nullifiers of Tawhid can be broadly categorized into matters of belief and matters of practice, though the two are inseparably intertwined. The gravest nullifier is major shirk — directing any act of worship to other than Allah. This includes supplication (du'a), sacrifice (dhabh), vows (nadhr), fear, love, hope, and reliance when directed exclusively to other than Allah and at the level that is due only to Allah. The Quran states: 'Indeed, whoever associates partners with Allah, Allah has forbidden Paradise for him, and his abode is the Fire, and for the wrongdoers there are no helpers' (Quran 5:72).

Among the categories that Ibn Abd al-Wahhab addresses with particular care is the practice of swearing oaths by other than Allah. The Prophet ﷺ said: 'Whoever swears by other than Allah has committed shirk' (Abu Dawud, al-Tirmidhi, deemed authentic). Swearing an oath is an act that implicitly attributes a level of sanctity and sovereignty to the one sworn by — it is a recognition that the named being has knowledge of the truth and authority over the situation. When this is directed to other than Allah, it constitutes at minimum minor shirk (al-shirk al-asghar) according to most scholars, and may rise to major shirk depending on the nature of the intent.

The chapter also covers the matter of amulets and charms (tamimah). The Prophet ﷺ said: 'Whoever hangs an amulet has committed shirk.' (Ahmad, with an authentic chain.) The scholars have distinguished between amulets containing Quranic verses — on which there is scholarly disagreement as to permissibility — and those containing unknown symbols, names of jinn, or non-Quranic formulas, which are unanimously condemned. The concern is not merely superstition but the theological assumption underlying the use of such objects: the belief that a physical object has inherent power to protect, heal, or attract — a power that belongs to Allah alone.

Riya' (ostentation) — performing acts of worship to be seen by people rather than for the sake of Allah — is another major threat to Tawhid identified in this chapter. It does not necessarily nullify Tawhid entirely unless a person's religion becomes entirely oriented toward seeking human approval. But when it contaminates an act of worship, it voids that particular act's spiritual reward. The Prophet ﷺ described riya' as 'the hidden shirk' (al-shirk al-khafi), and warned his Companions of it more than he warned them of the Dajjal (Antichrist) — because the Dajjal comes at a known time, but hidden ostentation is ever-present.

The final lesson of this chapter, and indeed of Kitab al-Tawhid as a foundational work, is that Tawhid is not a fixed state achieved once and maintained passively. It is a dynamic orientation of the heart that must be guarded, renewed, and purified continuously. The human soul is naturally inclined toward attachment to created things, toward seeking security in tangible objects and powerful people, and toward performing publicly what should be done privately for Allah. Tawhid demands vigilance — not of despair, but of a conscious and ongoing return to the recognition that Allah alone is sufficient, that He alone is worthy of worship, and that no other power, being, or system has any claim on the human heart's ultimate loyalty.
